California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 23,
1993
Peter Bagatelos
BAGATELOS
&
FADEM
601 California Street
Suite 1801
San Francisco,
CA 94108
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Dear Mr. Bagatelos:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you pursuant to the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”)
.
The advice was requested on behalf of San Francisco
Supervisor Angela Alioto concerning her proposed trip to China.
This letter confirms the oral advice I gave Marc Provissiero and
summarizes the three telephone conversations we had subsequent to
the receipt of your initial letter of August 10,
1993.2
In
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
As I advised you in September (see below), your letter of
August 10,
1993, requesting confirmation of the oral advice I had
provided to Marc Provissiero during the week of July 12,
1993, is
not entirely accurate.
Mr. Provissiero asked us four questions,
namely,
(1)
May Supervisor Alioto accept payment of her travel,
including actual domestic transportation and related lodging and
subsistence within China;
(2)
Is there a difference between
Supervisor Alioto and the persons she designates to accompany her;
(3)
May her staff or campaign workers who will be accompanying her
on the trip receive gifts; and
(4)
May her Friends Account or
campaign pay for the travel and lodging expenses for her staff and
campaign workers who will be accompanying her on the trip.
Briefly,
I answered
(1)
Yes,
18950.2(c);
(2)
Yes,
Supervisor
Alioto is the source of the gifts for persons accompanying her
(Regulation 18945)
whereas foreign country is the source of the
gifts to her;
(3)
Yes, Regulation 18950.2(b);
(4) It depends upon
specific person,
Section 89513(a)
or Section 89513(f).
In your initial letter of August 10,
1993,
you rephrased the
initial inquiry, consolidated the four questions into three
questions,
and thus did not accurately summarize the oral advice
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93--441
Page
2
addition, this letter shall serve as a reply to your most recent
written request for advice, our file number A-93-441.
This letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ocrlesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
While you did provide this
office with supplemental information and argument as to why
Supervisor Alioto should be permitted to use her campaign funds to
pay the travel expenses of Mr. Mitch Fine so that he could
accompany her on the trip to China, at no time did you provide us
with an itinerary or a proposed schedule of activities for
Supervisor Alioto or any of her entourage.
Therefore,
our reply
to your questions concerning gifts and use of campaign funds is
necessarily limited.
QUESTIONS
1.
May Angela Alioto accept the Chengdu Province
Government’s offer to pay for the travel and related costs of
Angela Alioto and four persons designated by her?
2.
How should such payments be reported by Supervisor
Alioto,
by her staff,
by other city employees,
and by other
members of the San Francisco community?
Original 3.
Can Angela Alioto’s officeholder account be used
to pay for any portion of the expenses of this trip for
(a) Angela
Alioto,
(b)
members of her household who live with her,
(C) the
head of the San Francisco Zoo,
(d)
members of the press,
(e)
members of the business community,
(f)
members of the San
Francisco Zoological Society,
a nonprofit organization organized
to help support the Zoo,
(g)
Angela Alioto’s press secretary
(campaign staff),
and
(h) her other friends and supporters?
Revised 3.
May Angela Alioto use campaign funds in her
officeholder account to pay for any portion of the expenses of
this trip for
(a)
Angela Alioto,
(b) her mother
(who lives with
her),
(c)
Mr.
Hal Markowitz
(Director of Research at the San
Francisco Zoo),
(d)
Marc Provissiero (her campaign staff press
secretary),
(e)
Mr. Francisco Hsieh (her liason to the Asian
community),
and
(f)
Mr. Mitch Fine (her legal and political
advisor)?
given during the week of July 12,
1993.
We discussed the various
issues on four different occasions in an attempt to clarify any
misunderstandings,
namely,
on August 3,
1993,
and again on
September 13,
17,
and 21 or 22,
1993.
Those conversations
resulted in additional information being submitted to this office
by facsimile on September 14 and 17 and again on November 12,
1993,
by a separate request for written advice,
our file number
A-93—441, consolidated in our reply herein.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93441
Page
3
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Supervisor Alioto may accept the Chengdu Province
Government’s offer to pay for her travel,
including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence during her trip
to China.
The payment of these travel expenses on her behalf are
not subject to the statutory $1,000 gift limit of the Act.
Moreover, Supervisor Alioto may accept the Chengdu Province
Government’s offer to pay for the travel expenses of four persons
designated by her to accompany her on the trip to China.
However,
these payments will be subject to the $1,000 gift limit.
3
2.
Regardless of whether a gift is subject to the $1,000
limit or exempt from the limit, absent an express exception to the
definition of “gift” in Section 82028, the receipt of free
transportation,
lodging,
and reasonable subsistence would be
disciosable on Supervisor Alioto’s anriual.Statement of Economic
Interests.
The donor is the Chengdu Province Government.
Likewise,
any of the four persons who are designated by
Supervisor Alioto to receive free transportation,
lodging,
and
reasonable subsistence within China would disclose these gifts on
their annual Statement of Economic Interests, if they are public
officials under the Act.
The donor is Angela Alioto.
3.
Supervisor Alioto may use campaign funds to pay for her
own transportation expenses to China as well as for the
transportation expenses to China of her mother,
Marc Provissiero,
Hal Markowitz, Francisco Hsieh and Mitch Fine.
However,
she may
not use campaign funds to pay for all of their expenses, but only
for those expenses that are directly related to a political,
legislative,
or governmental purpose.
FACTS
Facts Provided In Initial Letter Of Auqust 10,
1993
The Government of Chengdu Province in China has invited
Angela Alioto to attend the Panda Festival in Chengdu.
The
festival is from September 24 to September 28,
1993,
and the total
Your most recent letter (dated November 12,
1993)
indicates
that Supervisor Alioto accepted five tickets for travel within
China for herself and her entourage.
You do not disclose the
value of these tickets or of the hotel accomodations or food.
However, it would appear that the value of the tickets,
food,
and
lodging for her entourage exceeded the statutory $1,000 limit.
You did not ask, but it appears you presumed that the gift limit
was not placed on gifts of travel provided by Chengdu Province to
Supervisor Alioto for her entourage.
Since this point was not
clarified during our telephone conversations,
we will permit
Supervisor Alioto to use her campaign funds to reimburse the
Chengdu Government within 30 days of receipt of this letter.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Page 4
time period of the proposed trip is nine days.
The Chengdu
Province Government has offered to pay for all expenses, including
domestic transportation from the arriving city to Chengdu,
round
trip tickets,
and nine days of hotel,
food,
and transportation for
Supervisor Alioto and four additional people to be designated by
her.
They have not offered to pay for the airfare from the United
States to China and return.
Angela Alioto is planning on taking
an entourage of
15—30 people, consisting of herself, her mother
and son, both who live with her,
Marc Provissiero, her press
secretary and campaign fundraiser,
a public relations person
associated with the Asian community, approximately six Chinese
businessmen from San Francisco,
two or three press representatives
from San Francisco,
San Francisco Zoological Society members,
an
employee of the San Francisco Zoo,
and other supporters of Angela
Alioto.
Angela Alioto currently maintains an officeholder account for
her current term of office from which she pays expenses associated
with holding office.
Angela Alioto previously introduced proposals to attempt to
secure rare Chinese Pandas for the San Francisco Zoo.
In response
to her communications with representatives of the Chengdu Province
Government,
she has been invited to China to explore the
possibility of obtaining such Panda bears.
It is also
contemplated that,
as part of her trip,
she will explore some type
of formal relationship between San Francisco and the Chengdu
Province,
such as a friendship or a sister city,
as well as the
possibility of developing trade opportunities between the two
jurisdictions.
Facts Provided In Supplemental Letter Of September 14,
1993
Dr.
Hal Markowitz is the Director of Research at the San
Francisco Zoo.
He is a city/county employee.
Mr.
Francisco Hsieh is a member of the Chinese Chamber of
Commerce.
He is a fundraiser for Supervisor Alioto and acts as
her liaison to the Asian community.
He is familiar with Chinese
languages and protocols.
Mr.
Mitch Fine is an attorney.
He is the treasurer and
finance director of one of Angela Alioto’s committees, Alioto for
Mayor 1991.
He is a former chairperson of the budget committee of
the Democratic Party and has strong political ties to prominent
Democrats.
He is a key political adviser to Supervisor Alioto.
Facts Provided In Second Supplemental Letter Of September 17,
1993
The purpose of the trip includes developing stronger
political ties between the City and County of San Francisco and
the Chinese governments,
encouraging business and trade
connections that would benefit San Francisco,
and promoting and
developing communications consistent with the interests of,
and


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Page 5
which would benefit, the City and County of San Francisco.
While
the issue of Panda bears has taken prominence as a primary reason
for the trip, there are definitely other principal purposes for
the trip,
including the trade and political components.
Mitch Fine is an attorney,
a businessman,
and a financial and
political consultant to Supervisor Alioto.
Supervisor Alioto’s
officeholder account intends to retain Mr. Fine to act as
Supervisor Alioto’s legal and political advisor on the trip to
China.
In this connection,
campaign funds will be used to
remunerate Mr. Fine for his legal services as well as to pay him
for his travel expenses to China.
For example,
a principal issue
will be negotiations as to terms of an agreement for delivery of
Panda bears to the San Francisco Zoo.
Mr. Fine will assist in
those discussions and negotiations and advise Supervisor Alioto as
to applicable legal considerations.
One of the subissues related to the Panda bears is that the
Secretary of the Interior,
Bruce Babbitt, recently issued a
directive that zoos in the United States would lose their
accreditation if they were to receive Panda bears from China.
Apparently, there is concern that the sale of Panda bears by the
Chinese governments is resulting in the proceeds not being used to
promote Panda bear preservation, but rather to benefit certain
private or individual interests.
Given Mr. Fine’s connections
with the Democratic Party,
he could learn about the legal issues
affecting Panda bear delivery at the China end of the trip and
then meet and communicate with Mr. Babbitt and his staff,
so that
he could assist Supervisor Alioto in formulating an agreement that
would satisfy everyone’s interests.
Mr. Fine serves as a member of,
and financial expert to, the
Lead Hazard Reduction Committee,
an advisory committee established
by the San Francisco Board of Supervisors.
Lead poisoning is
caused by most paints and there are very few alternative non—toxic
paint products available in the United States.
Mr.
Fine states
that there are resins located in China, not available in the
United States, which may lead to the development of non-toxic
natural based paints.
As part of his trip,
Mr.
Fine would meet
with experts in China regarding those alternative products and
issue a report upon his return to the advisory committee.
Supervisor Alioto had a strong role in establishing this
committee.
Supervisor Alioto has a continuing,
long—term relationship
with Mr.
Fine.
He will serve as her legal and political advisor
for all political matters that may affect her status as an
officeholder and candidate during the trip.
For example, he will
advise her on the legally permissible use of campaign funds under
the Political Reform Act.


Our File Nos.
A—93-309/A—93—441
Page
6
FollowuD Letter Of November 12,
1993
(Our File No.
93-441)
Supervisor Alioto was given five tickets for travel within
China for herself and her entourage.
Supervisor Alioto offered
one of the tickets to Mitch Fine to act in the capacity of a legal
and financial advisor for the trip.
He was, in fact, retained for
that purpose.
Mr. Fine is an attorney and a businessman in San
Francisco.
He is familiar with the Chinese language and culture.
He has also served as treasurer of Angela Alioto’s mayoral
committee, as a finance director for her various committees,
and a
close legal advisor to her on campaign, elections,
and
officeholder matters.
The Alioto delegation’s first destination was Shanghai, the
sister city of San Francisco, where their goal was to explore
trade opportunities, specifically within Shanghai’s “Pudong New
Area” and subarea Jingiao Export Processing Zone.
In 1992, United
States’ contracts for the area totalled $1.6 billion.
Mr. Fine
assisted in arranging top level meetings between Supervisor Alioto
and the Director of the Foreign Economy Commission,
Lhang Yi Ru,
and the Deputy Chief of the District People’s Government,
Shen Mm
He.
On their second day in Shanghai,
a letter of intent was
signed between Charles Condy,
CEO of Proven Alternatives
(located
in San Francisco)
and also a member of Supervisor Alioto’s
delegation,
and the District Government to explore natural gas
development.
Mr. Fine was present during these meetings.
He
advised Supervisor Alioto on the political and economic structure
of Pudong and on the approval procedures concerning overseas
funded ventures in the Pudong New Area.
Their next destination was Beijing, where one of their
purposes was to explore and develop the possibility of bringing a
Panda from a Chinese Zoo to San Francisco and to work with the
Chinese to encourage the protection of Panda wildlife conservation
areas.
There are complex issues surrounding the import of Pandas to
the United States.
First, Secretary of the Interior, Bruce
Babbitt, has forbidden any such importation.
Second, negotiations
with the Government of China have to be conducted in a multi-
tiered manner; application must be submitted to the Central
Government in Beijing for approval and to the regional government
in Chengdu for implementation.
The United States must be assured
that any Panda importation is clearly tied to a wildlife
conservation program in Si Chuan.
One member of the Alioto delegation,
Hal Markowitz,
was key
to this process.
Mr. Markowitz, the Director of Research at the
San Francisco Zoo,
and Mr.
Fine worked out a program that merged
all concerns so that Supervisor Alioto could discuss and work
through the various obstacles in her presentations to the Central
Committee in Beijing and to the mayor of Chengdu.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93--441
Page 7
The highlight of the trip occurred in Chengdu, the final
destination of their trip.
On the eve of the International Panda
Festival,
Mayor Ynkui Huang entered into a covenant with
Supervisor Alioto that San Francisco would receive the highest
priority in obtaining a Panda once the United States Government
and the Chinese Central Committee had worked out the terms of such
an arrangement.
Mr. Fine helped formulate the details of the
covenant.
Various other meetings occurred during the trip involving
economic and political issues.
Mr. Fine advised Supervisor Alioto
regarding each of the issues that were raised during those
meetings.
ANALYSIS
Question 1:
May Supervisor Angela Alioto accept the Chengdu
Province Government’s offer to pay for the travel and related
costs of Angela Alioto and four persons designated by her?
The Act imposes different obligations on public officials
regarding the receipt of gifts.
First,
Section 89501 provides a
gift limit for local elected officials.
Second, the Act requires
that every public official disclose all his or her economic
interests that could foreseeably be financially affected by the
exercise of the official’s duties.
(Sections 81002(c),
87207,
87302.)
Third,
Section 87100 requires that public officials
disqualify themselves from any governmental decision that will
have a foreseeable and material financial effect on a donor of
(or
any intermediary or agent for a donor of) gifts aggregating $250
or more in value provided to, received by,
or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103(e).
As we discussed in our telephone conversation, the Act now
provides a gift limit for local elected officials.
Section 89501
provides:
No local elected officeholder shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering,
or any gifts,
from any single source,
which. is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000),
in any calendar year,
except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
According to Section 89501,
local elected officers may not
receive honoraria and gifts aggregating more than $1,000 from any
single source in a calendar year.
However,
Section 89501 exempts
gifts of actual travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in
connection with attendance at any public or private conference,


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Page 8
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering,
from
the $1,000 gift limit of Section 89501.
(See also, Regulation
18950.2(b).)
The Commission further interprets the exemption to apply to
gifts of actual travel expenses, including actual transportation
and related lodging and subsistence if:
(1)
The travel is reasonably related to a
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue
of state, national, or international public policy;
and
(2)
The payment is provided by a government,
a governmental agency,
a foreign government,
a
governmental authority
(Regulation 18950.2(c).)
The gift limits of the Act only apply to local elected
officers, elected state officers, designated employees of state
agencies,
and members of state boards of commissions.
Thus,
a
person who does not hold one of those positions is not subject to
a statutory gift limit.
From the facts provided in your letters,
it seems that the gift limit would only apply to Supervisor
Alioto, since none of the other four persons accompanying her on
the trip to China appear to be persons to whom a gift limit would
apply.
(Sections 89501,
89504,
89505.)
Supervisor Alioto’s travel, including actual transportation
and related lodging and subsistence, is being paid for by a
foreign government, the Government of Chengdu Province, China.
These travel expenses are being provided to her so that she may
meet with members of the Chinese government to explore the
possibility of obtaining Panda bears and some type of formal
relationship between San Francisco and the Chengdu Province,
such
as a friendship or a sister city, as well as the possibility of
developing trade opportunities between the two jurisdictions.
Such meetings appear to be reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or
international public policy.
Consequently, under your facts, the
payments for Supervisor Alioto’s transportation and related
lodging and subsistence are not subject to the $1,000 gift limit
of Section 89501.
-
However, the Government of Chengdu Province, China, has also
offered to pay for the actual transportation and related lodging
and subsistence of four persons-designated by Supervisor Alioto to
accompany her on her trip to China.
As I previously advised,
we
consider these payments to be gifts to Supervisor Alioto.
(Regulation 18945.)
Unlike China’s payment of Supervisor Alioto’s
actual travel expenses, these gifts are subject to the $1,000
limit.
Also, payments by the Chengdu Province Government for


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Page 9
anything other than Supervisor Alioto’s actual travel expenses are
subject to the $1,000 limit.
The payment of travel expenses for Supervisor Alioto’s
mother,
Marc Provissiero,
Francisco Hsieh,
and Hal Markowitz)
4 are
gifts to them from Supervisor Alioto but are not subject to the
gift limit of the Act, unless the recipient is one to whom the
gift limit would apply.
If that is the case, the payments for
their travel expenses still are not subject to the gift limit,
provided the payments are made in connection with the their
attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.
(Regulation
18950.2.)
Question 2:
How should such payments be reported by Supervisor
Alioto,
by her staff,
by other city employees,
and by other
members of the San Francisco community?
Regardless of whether a gift is subject to the limit or
exempt from the limit, absent an express exception to the
definition of “gift” in Section 82028, the receipt of free
transportation,
lodging,
and reasonable subsistence would be
disciosable on Supervisor Alioto’s annual Statement of Economic
Interests.
(Section 82028 (b) (1).)
The donor in her case is the
Chengdu Province Government.
Likewise,
any of the persons in Supervisor Alioto’s entourage
who is a public official under the Act and who receives free
transportation,
lodging,
and reasonable subsistence in connection
with his or her attendance at a conference or other similar
gathering would disclose these gifts on their annual Statements of
Economic Interests.
The donor in their case is Angela Alioto.
Also,
as we discussed,
Section 87100,
g.,
of the Act
requires that public officials who have received gifts of $250 or
more from a single source must disqualify themselves from all
governmental decisions that will foreseeably and materially affect
the donor.
If a decision should come before Supervisor Alioto,
Mr. Markowitz,
or any other public official accompanying her,
in
the future concerning the Chengdu Province Government,
she or he
We were told that the Chengdu Province Government offered to
pay for the travel expenses of four additional persons, other than
Supervisor Alioto, to be designated by her.
However, in your last
correspondence,
you added the name of a fifth person, Mitch Fine.
Note, unlike Supervisor Alioto’s travel expenses which are paid
for by a governmental entity and are exempt from the $1,000 gift
limit on those grounds
(Regulation 18950.2 (c)), •the travel
expenses of the four (or five) persons accompanying her are paid
for by Supervisor Alioto.
Thus,
in order for their travel
expenses to be exempt from the gift limit, the test set forth in
Regulation 18950.2(b)
must be met.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Page 10
should contact us for advice about potential conflicts of
interest.
Question 3:
May Angela Alioto use campaign funds in her
officeholder account to pay for any portion of the expenses of
this trip for
(a)
Angela Alioto,
(b) her mother
(who lives with
her),
(c)
Mr. Hal Markowitz, the Director of Research at the San
Francisco Zoo
(a city/county employee),
(d)
Marc Provissiero, her
press secretary,
(e)
Mr. Francisco Hsieh,
and (f)
Mr. Mitch Fine?
Effective January 1,
1990, the Act was aiuended by Senate
Bill 1431 to include new provisions that regulate the appropriate
use of campaign funds.
(Formerly Sections 85800,
sea.;
now
Sections 89510—89521.)
The purpose of the legislation was “to
ensure that candidates, elected officers and the people clearly
recognize acceptable and unacceptable uses of campaign funds.”
(Senate Elections Committee Analysis of SB 1431.)
The general rule of the personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 89512.)
However, where an expenditure confers a
substantial personal benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the
expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose.
(Section 89512.)
A substantial personal
benefit is defined as a direct personal benefit to the candidate
or officeholder of $100 or more.
(Section 89511(b) (3).)
Moreover, the personal use law sets forth provisions dealing
with specific types of expenditures.
Section 89513(a), which
deals with travel, provides:
(a)
Campaign funds shall not be used to pay
or reimburse the candidate, the elected officer, or
any individual or individuals with authority to
approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee,
or employees or staff of the committee
or the elected officer’s governmental agency for
travel expenses and necessary accommodations except
when these expenditures are directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(1)
For the purposes of this section,
payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary
accommodations shall be considered as directly
related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose if the payments would meet
standards similar to the standards of the Internal
Revenue Service pursuant to Sections 162 and 274 of
the Internal Revenue Code for deductions of travel
expenses under the federal income tax law.
(2)
For the purposes of this section,
payments or reimbursement for travel by the


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Page 11
household of a candidate or elected officer when
traveling to the same destination in order to
accompany the candidate or elected officer shall be
considered for the same purpose as the candidate’s
or elected officer’s travel.
According to Section 89513 (a) (1), the use of campaign funds
for payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary
accommodations is permissible if the payments meet standards
similar to the standards for the deduction of travel expenses
pursuant to Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and aZ4.
Until the
Commission adopts regulations,
we are interpreting Section
-89513(a) (1) to require a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose for the travel expenses that are clearly associated with a
governmental purpose, the elective office held,
or the elective
office sought by the official.
If there is a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose,
campaign funds may be used
for ordinary and necessary expenses associated with the travel and
accommodation consistent with the Internal Revenue Service
standards.
Though the Commission has not yet defined a
“governmental purpose” under the personal use sections, it appears
from the information you have provided that the trip is directly
related to a governmental purpose.
6
Internal Revenue Code Section 162(a) (2)
sets forth general
rules which permit the deduction of all ordinary and necessary
traveling expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade
or business.
In light of the purpose behind the personal use law,
we have interpreted the Internal Revenue Code Sections tà allow
only for the use of campaign funds for the “business portions” of
the trip.
(Bass Advice Letter,
No. A-90-474.)
Thus, the trip to
China must be broken into business days
(days on which there are
meetings)
and nonbusiness days.
Where a weekend falls between
business days, the weekend will also be counted as business time.
However,
no part of expenditures for solely personal purposes,
such as some of the meals, entertainment, guided cultural and
sight-seeing activities,
may be paid for with campaign funds.
Since Section 89513(a)
applies in pertinent part to the
elected officer, household members of the elected officer,
and to
employees or staff of the committee or of the elected officer’s
governmental agency, it would be permissible for Supervisor Alioto
to pay for her own transportation expenses to China,
as well as
for the transportation expenses to China of her mother,
Marc
Provissiero (press secretary on her campaign staff),
and Hal
Markowitz
(assuming he is an employee of the City and County of
San Francisco).
7
6
However, without an itinerary or a proposed schedule of
activities,
we cannot comment as to what portions of the trip are
related to a governmental purpose.
Otherwise, he would qualify under Section 89513(f)
below.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Page 12
Section 89513 (f), which deals with gifts, appears to apply to
Francisco Hsieh and Mitch Fine.
8
Section 89513(f) (1) uses the
same test for permissible expenditures for gifts.
The section
provides that “[c]ampaign funds shall not be used to make personal
gifts unless the gift is directly related to a political,
legislative,
or governmental purpose.”.
Thus, based on all the
information you have provided, it appears that Supervisor Alioto
may use campaign funds to pay for the travel expenses of Francisco
Hsieh and Mitch Fine to China.
9
Section 89513 (b), which deals with expenditures for
professional services, also applies to Mitch Fine.
The section
provides that “[c)ainpaign funds shall not be used to pay for or
reimburse the cost of professional services unless the services
are directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.”
Based on the information you provided in your letter of
November 12,
1993, the committee may pay for the legal services of
Mr. Fine.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59O1.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
-
General Counsel
By:
Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel
8
At the time of your letters and our telephone conversations,
Mr. Mitch Fine was not on Supervisor Alioto’s campaign staff.
Later, after our telephone conversations, he was retained by
Supervisor Alioto to accompany her on the China trip.
However,
your letter of November 12,
1993, still does not clarify whether
Mr.
Fine is a member of her campaign staff or a professional who
was retained for this limited purpose.
For purposes of our
letter,
we will continue to assume that Mr.
Fine is not a campaign
staffer.
You have indicated that Supervisor Alioto intends to select
both Mr. Hsieh and Mr.
Fine as recipients of the gifts of travel
within China.
Therefore,
we have not explored, nor were we asked,
to consider the permissibility of the expenditure of campaign
funds for any other purpose.
10
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of iOc per page.

sections.conclusion:
1.
Supervisor Alioto may accept the Chengdu Province
Government’s offer to pay for her travel,
including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence during her trip
to China.
The payment of these travel expenses on her behalf are
not subject to the statutory $1,000 gift limit of the Act.
Moreover, Supervisor Alioto may accept the Chengdu Province
Government’s offer to pay for the travel expenses of four persons
designated by her to accompany her on the trip to China.
However,
these payments will be subject to the $1,000 gift limit.
3
2.
Regardless of whether a gift is subject to the $1,000
limit or exempt from the limit, absent an express exception to the
definition of “gift” in Section 82028, the receipt of free
transportation,
lodging,
and reasonable subsistence would be
disciosable on Supervisor Alioto’s anriual.Statement of Economic
Interests.
The donor is the Chengdu Province Government.
Likewise,
any of the four persons who are designated by
Supervisor Alioto to receive free transportation,
lodging,
and
reasonable subsistence within China would disclose these gifts on
their annual Statement of Economic Interests, if they are public
officials under the Act.
The donor is Angela Alioto.
3.
Supervisor Alioto may use campaign funds to pay for her
own transportation expenses to China as well as for the
transportation expenses to China of her mother,
Marc Provissiero,
Hal Markowitz, Francisco Hsieh and Mitch Fine.
However,
she may
not use campaign funds to pay for all of their expenses, but only
for those expenses that are directly related to a political,
legislative,
or governmental purpose.
sections.facts:
Facts Provided In Initial Letter Of Auqust 10,
1993
The Government of Chengdu Province in China has invited
Angela Alioto to attend the Panda Festival in Chengdu.
The
festival is from September 24 to September 28,
1993,
and the total
Your most recent letter (dated November 12,
1993)
indicates
that Supervisor Alioto accepted five tickets for travel within
China for herself and her entourage.
You do not disclose the
value of these tickets or of the hotel accomodations or food.
However, it would appear that the value of the tickets,
food,
and
lodging for her entourage exceeded the statutory $1,000 limit.
You did not ask, but it appears you presumed that the gift limit
was not placed on gifts of travel provided by Chengdu Province to
Supervisor Alioto for her entourage.
Since this point was not
clarified during our telephone conversations,
we will permit
Supervisor Alioto to use her campaign funds to reimburse the
Chengdu Government within 30 days of receipt of this letter.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
time period of the proposed trip is nine days.
The Chengdu
Province Government has offered to pay for all expenses, including
domestic transportation from the arriving city to Chengdu,
round
trip tickets,
and nine days of hotel,
food,
and transportation for
Supervisor Alioto and four additional people to be designated by
her.
They have not offered to pay for the airfare from the United
States to China and return.
Angela Alioto is planning on taking
an entourage of
15—30 people, consisting of herself, her mother
and son, both who live with her,
Marc Provissiero, her press
secretary and campaign fundraiser,
a public relations person
associated with the Asian community, approximately six Chinese
businessmen from San Francisco,
two or three press representatives
from San Francisco,
San Francisco Zoological Society members,
an
employee of the San Francisco Zoo,
and other supporters of Angela
Alioto.
Angela Alioto currently maintains an officeholder account for
her current term of office from which she pays expenses associated
with holding office.
Angela Alioto previously introduced proposals to attempt to
secure rare Chinese Pandas for the San Francisco Zoo.
In response
to her communications with representatives of the Chengdu Province
Government,
she has been invited to China to explore the
possibility of obtaining such Panda bears.
It is also
contemplated that,
as part of her trip,
she will explore some type
of formal relationship between San Francisco and the Chengdu
Province,
such as a friendship or a sister city,
as well as the
possibility of developing trade opportunities between the two
jurisdictions.
Facts Provided In Supplemental Letter Of September 14,
1993
Dr.
Hal Markowitz is the Director of Research at the San
Francisco Zoo.
He is a city/county employee.
Mr.
Francisco Hsieh is a member of the Chinese Chamber of
Commerce.
He is a fundraiser for Supervisor Alioto and acts as
her liaison to the Asian community.
He is familiar with Chinese
languages and protocols.
Mr.
Mitch Fine is an attorney.
He is the treasurer and
finance director of one of Angela Alioto’s committees, Alioto for
Mayor 1991.
He is a former chairperson of the budget committee of
the Democratic Party and has strong political ties to prominent
Democrats.
He is a key political adviser to Supervisor Alioto.
Facts Provided In Second Supplemental Letter Of September 17,
1993
The purpose of the trip includes developing stronger
political ties between the City and County of San Francisco and
the Chinese governments,
encouraging business and trade
connections that would benefit San Francisco,
and promoting and
developing communications consistent with the interests of,
and


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
which would benefit, the City and County of San Francisco.
While
the issue of Panda bears has taken prominence as a primary reason
for the trip, there are definitely other principal purposes for
the trip,
including the trade and political components.
Mitch Fine is an attorney,
a businessman,
and a financial and
political consultant to Supervisor Alioto.
Supervisor Alioto’s
officeholder account intends to retain Mr. Fine to act as
Supervisor Alioto’s legal and political advisor on the trip to
China.
In this connection,
campaign funds will be used to
remunerate Mr. Fine for his legal services as well as to pay him
for his travel expenses to China.
For example,
a principal issue
will be negotiations as to terms of an agreement for delivery of
Panda bears to the San Francisco Zoo.
Mr. Fine will assist in
those discussions and negotiations and advise Supervisor Alioto as
to applicable legal considerations.
One of the subissues related to the Panda bears is that the
Secretary of the Interior,
Bruce Babbitt, recently issued a
directive that zoos in the United States would lose their
accreditation if they were to receive Panda bears from China.
Apparently, there is concern that the sale of Panda bears by the
Chinese governments is resulting in the proceeds not being used to
promote Panda bear preservation, but rather to benefit certain
private or individual interests.
Given Mr. Fine’s connections
with the Democratic Party,
he could learn about the legal issues
affecting Panda bear delivery at the China end of the trip and
then meet and communicate with Mr. Babbitt and his staff,
so that
he could assist Supervisor Alioto in formulating an agreement that
would satisfy everyone’s interests.
Mr. Fine serves as a member of,
and financial expert to, the
Lead Hazard Reduction Committee,
an advisory committee established
by the San Francisco Board of Supervisors.
Lead poisoning is
caused by most paints and there are very few alternative non—toxic
paint products available in the United States.
Mr.
Fine states
that there are resins located in China, not available in the
United States, which may lead to the development of non-toxic
natural based paints.
As part of his trip,
Mr.
Fine would meet
with experts in China regarding those alternative products and
issue a report upon his return to the advisory committee.
Supervisor Alioto had a strong role in establishing this
committee.
Supervisor Alioto has a continuing,
long—term relationship
with Mr.
Fine.
He will serve as her legal and political advisor
for all political matters that may affect her status as an
officeholder and candidate during the trip.
For example, he will
advise her on the legally permissible use of campaign funds under
the Political Reform Act.


Our File Nos.
A—93-309/A—93—441
FollowuD Letter Of November 12,
1993
(Our )
Supervisor Alioto was given five tickets for travel within
China for herself and her entourage.
Supervisor Alioto offered
one of the tickets to Mitch Fine to act in the capacity of a legal
and financial advisor for the trip.
He was, in fact, retained for
that purpose.
Mr. Fine is an attorney and a businessman in San
Francisco.
He is familiar with the Chinese language and culture.
He has also served as treasurer of Angela Alioto’s mayoral
committee, as a finance director for her various committees,
and a
close legal advisor to her on campaign, elections,
and
officeholder matters.
The Alioto delegation’s first destination was Shanghai, the
sister city of San Francisco, where their goal was to explore
trade opportunities, specifically within Shanghai’s “Pudong New
Area” and subarea Jingiao Export Processing Zone.
In 1992, United
States’ contracts for the area totalled $1.6 billion.
Mr. Fine
assisted in arranging top level meetings between Supervisor Alioto
and the Director of the Foreign Economy Commission,
Lhang Yi Ru,
and the Deputy Chief of the District People’s Government,
Shen Mm
He.
On their second day in Shanghai,
a letter of intent was
signed between Charles Condy,
CEO of Proven Alternatives
(located
in San Francisco)
and also a member of Supervisor Alioto’s
delegation,
and the District Government to explore natural gas
development.
Mr. Fine was present during these meetings.
He
advised Supervisor Alioto on the political and economic structure
of Pudong and on the approval procedures concerning overseas
funded ventures in the Pudong New Area.
Their next destination was Beijing, where one of their
purposes was to explore and develop the possibility of bringing a
Panda from a Chinese Zoo to San Francisco and to work with the
Chinese to encourage the protection of Panda wildlife conservation
areas.
There are complex issues surrounding the import of Pandas to
the United States.
First, Secretary of the Interior, Bruce
Babbitt, has forbidden any such importation.
Second, negotiations
with the Government of China have to be conducted in a multi-
tiered manner; application must be submitted to the Central
Government in Beijing for approval and to the regional government
in Chengdu for implementation.
The United States must be assured
that any Panda importation is clearly tied to a wildlife
conservation program in Si Chuan.
One member of the Alioto delegation,
Hal Markowitz,
was key
to this process.
Mr. Markowitz, the Director of Research at the
San Francisco Zoo,
and Mr.
Fine worked out a program that merged
all concerns so that Supervisor Alioto could discuss and work
through the various obstacles in her presentations to the Central
Committee in Beijing and to the mayor of Chengdu.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93--441
The highlight of the trip occurred in Chengdu, the final
destination of their trip.
On the eve of the International Panda
Festival,
Mayor Ynkui Huang entered into a covenant with
Supervisor Alioto that San Francisco would receive the highest
priority in obtaining a Panda once the United States Government
and the Chinese Central Committee had worked out the terms of such
an arrangement.
Mr. Fine helped formulate the details of the
covenant.
Various other meetings occurred during the trip involving
economic and political issues.
Mr. Fine advised Supervisor Alioto
regarding each of the issues that were raised during those
meetings.
sections.analysis:
Question 1:
May Supervisor Angela Alioto accept the Chengdu
Province Government’s offer to pay for the travel and related
costs of Angela Alioto and four persons designated by her?
The Act imposes different obligations on public officials
regarding the receipt of gifts.
First,
Section 89501 provides a
gift limit for local elected officials.
Second, the Act requires
that every public official disclose all his or her economic
interests that could foreseeably be financially affected by the
exercise of the official’s duties.
(Sections 81002(c),
87207,
87302.)
Third,
Section 87100 requires that public officials
disqualify themselves from any governmental decision that will
have a foreseeable and material financial effect on a donor of
(or
any intermediary or agent for a donor of) gifts aggregating $250
or more in value provided to, received by,
or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(Section 87103(e).
As we discussed in our telephone conversation, the Act now
provides a gift limit for local elected officials.
Section 89501
provides:
No local elected officeholder shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering,
or any gifts,
from any single source,
which. is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000),
in any calendar year,
except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
According to Section 89501,
local elected officers may not
receive honoraria and gifts aggregating more than $1,000 from any
single source in a calendar year.
However,
Section 89501 exempts
gifts of actual travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in
connection with attendance at any public or private conference,


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering,
from
the $1,000 gift limit of Section 89501.
(See also, Regulation
18950.2(b).)
The Commission further interprets the exemption to apply to
gifts of actual travel expenses, including actual transportation
and related lodging and subsistence if:
(1)
The travel is reasonably related to a
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue
of state, national, or international public policy;
and
(2)
The payment is provided by a government,
a governmental agency,
a foreign government,
a
governmental authority
(Regulation 18950.2(c).)
The gift limits of the Act only apply to local elected
officers, elected state officers, designated employees of state
agencies,
and members of state boards of commissions.
Thus,
a
person who does not hold one of those positions is not subject to
a statutory gift limit.
From the facts provided in your letters,
it seems that the gift limit would only apply to Supervisor
Alioto, since none of the other four persons accompanying her on
the trip to China appear to be persons to whom a gift limit would
apply.
(Sections 89501,
89504,
89505.)
Supervisor Alioto’s travel, including actual transportation
and related lodging and subsistence, is being paid for by a
foreign government, the Government of Chengdu Province, China.
These travel expenses are being provided to her so that she may
meet with members of the Chinese government to explore the
possibility of obtaining Panda bears and some type of formal
relationship between San Francisco and the Chengdu Province,
such
as a friendship or a sister city, as well as the possibility of
developing trade opportunities between the two jurisdictions.
Such meetings appear to be reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or
international public policy.
Consequently, under your facts, the
payments for Supervisor Alioto’s transportation and related
lodging and subsistence are not subject to the $1,000 gift limit
of Section 89501.
-
However, the Government of Chengdu Province, China, has also
offered to pay for the actual transportation and related lodging
and subsistence of four persons-designated by Supervisor Alioto to
accompany her on her trip to China.
As I previously advised,
we
consider these payments to be gifts to Supervisor Alioto.
(Regulation 18945.)
Unlike China’s payment of Supervisor Alioto’s
actual travel expenses, these gifts are subject to the $1,000
limit.
Also, payments by the Chengdu Province Government for


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
anything other than Supervisor Alioto’s actual travel expenses are
subject to the $1,000 limit.
The payment of travel expenses for Supervisor Alioto’s
mother,
Marc Provissiero,
Francisco Hsieh,
and Hal Markowitz)
4 are
gifts to them from Supervisor Alioto but are not subject to the
gift limit of the Act, unless the recipient is one to whom the
gift limit would apply.
If that is the case, the payments for
their travel expenses still are not subject to the gift limit,
provided the payments are made in connection with the their
attendance at any public or private conference, convention,
meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering.
(Regulation
18950.2.)
Question 2:
How should such payments be reported by Supervisor
Alioto,
by her staff,
by other city employees,
and by other
members of the San Francisco community?
Regardless of whether a gift is subject to the limit or
exempt from the limit, absent an express exception to the
definition of “gift” in Section 82028, the receipt of free
transportation,
lodging,
and reasonable subsistence would be
disciosable on Supervisor Alioto’s annual Statement of Economic
Interests.
(Section 82028 (b) (1).)
The donor in her case is the
Chengdu Province Government.
Likewise,
any of the persons in Supervisor Alioto’s entourage
who is a public official under the Act and who receives free
transportation,
lodging,
and reasonable subsistence in connection
with his or her attendance at a conference or other similar
gathering would disclose these gifts on their annual Statements of
Economic Interests.
The donor in their case is Angela Alioto.
Also,
as we discussed,
Section 87100,
g.,
of the Act
requires that public officials who have received gifts of $250 or
more from a single source must disqualify themselves from all
governmental decisions that will foreseeably and materially affect
the donor.
If a decision should come before Supervisor Alioto,
Mr. Markowitz,
or any other public official accompanying her,
in
the future concerning the Chengdu Province Government,
she or he
We were told that the Chengdu Province Government offered to
pay for the travel expenses of four additional persons, other than
Supervisor Alioto, to be designated by her.
However, in your last
correspondence,
you added the name of a fifth person, Mitch Fine.
Note, unlike Supervisor Alioto’s travel expenses which are paid
for by a governmental entity and are exempt from the $1,000 gift
limit on those grounds
(Regulation 18950.2 (c)), •the travel
expenses of the four (or five) persons accompanying her are paid
for by Supervisor Alioto.
Thus,
in order for their travel
expenses to be exempt from the gift limit, the test set forth in
Regulation 18950.2(b)
must be met.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
should contact us for advice about potential conflicts of
interest.
Question 3:
May Angela Alioto use campaign funds in her
officeholder account to pay for any portion of the expenses of
this trip for
(a)
Angela Alioto,
(b) her mother
(who lives with
her),
(c)
Mr. Hal Markowitz, the Director of Research at the San
Francisco Zoo
(a city/county employee),
(d)
Marc Provissiero, her
press secretary,
(e)
Mr. Francisco Hsieh,
and (f)
Mr. Mitch Fine?
Effective January 1,
1990, the Act was aiuended by Senate
Bill 1431 to include new provisions that regulate the appropriate
use of campaign funds.
(Formerly Sections 85800,
sea.;
now
Sections 89510—89521.)
The purpose of the legislation was “to
ensure that candidates, elected officers and the people clearly
recognize acceptable and unacceptable uses of campaign funds.”
(Senate Elections Committee Analysis of SB 1431.)
The general rule of the personal use law is that any
expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 89512.)
However, where an expenditure confers a
substantial personal benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the
expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose.
(Section 89512.)
A substantial personal
benefit is defined as a direct personal benefit to the candidate
or officeholder of $100 or more.
(Section 89511(b) (3).)
Moreover, the personal use law sets forth provisions dealing
with specific types of expenditures.
Section 89513(a), which
deals with travel, provides:
(a)
Campaign funds shall not be used to pay
or reimburse the candidate, the elected officer, or
any individual or individuals with authority to
approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee,
or employees or staff of the committee
or the elected officer’s governmental agency for
travel expenses and necessary accommodations except
when these expenditures are directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(1)
For the purposes of this section,
payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary
accommodations shall be considered as directly
related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose if the payments would meet
standards similar to the standards of the Internal
Revenue Service pursuant to Sections 162 and 274 of
the Internal Revenue Code for deductions of travel
expenses under the federal income tax law.
(2)
For the purposes of this section,
payments or reimbursement for travel by the


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
household of a candidate or elected officer when
traveling to the same destination in order to
accompany the candidate or elected officer shall be
considered for the same purpose as the candidate’s
or elected officer’s travel.
According to Section 89513 (a) (1), the use of campaign funds
for payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary
accommodations is permissible if the payments meet standards
similar to the standards for the deduction of travel expenses
pursuant to Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and aZ4.
Until the
Commission adopts regulations,
we are interpreting Section
-89513(a) (1) to require a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose for the travel expenses that are clearly associated with a
governmental purpose, the elective office held,
or the elective
office sought by the official.
If there is a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose,
campaign funds may be used
for ordinary and necessary expenses associated with the travel and
accommodation consistent with the Internal Revenue Service
standards.
Though the Commission has not yet defined a
“governmental purpose” under the personal use sections, it appears
from the information you have provided that the trip is directly
related to a governmental purpose.
6
Internal Revenue Code Section 162(a) (2)
sets forth general
rules which permit the deduction of all ordinary and necessary
traveling expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade
or business.
In light of the purpose behind the personal use law,
we have interpreted the Internal Revenue Code Sections tà allow
only for the use of campaign funds for the “business portions” of
the trip.
(Bass Advice Letter,
No. A-90-474.)
Thus, the trip to
China must be broken into business days
(days on which there are
meetings)
and nonbusiness days.
Where a weekend falls between
business days, the weekend will also be counted as business time.
However,
no part of expenditures for solely personal purposes,
such as some of the meals, entertainment, guided cultural and
sight-seeing activities,
may be paid for with campaign funds.
Since Section 89513(a)
applies in pertinent part to the
elected officer, household members of the elected officer,
and to
employees or staff of the committee or of the elected officer’s
governmental agency, it would be permissible for Supervisor Alioto
to pay for her own transportation expenses to China,
as well as
for the transportation expenses to China of her mother,
Marc
Provissiero (press secretary on her campaign staff),
and Hal
Markowitz
(assuming he is an employee of the City and County of
San Francisco).
7
6
However, without an itinerary or a proposed schedule of
activities,
we cannot comment as to what portions of the trip are
related to a governmental purpose.
Otherwise, he would qualify under Section 89513(f)
below.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93—441
Section 89513 (f), which deals with gifts, appears to apply to
Francisco Hsieh and Mitch Fine.
8
Section 89513(f) (1) uses the
same test for permissible expenditures for gifts.
The section
provides that “[c]ampaign funds shall not be used to make personal
gifts unless the gift is directly related to a political,
legislative,
or governmental purpose.”.
Thus, based on all the
information you have provided, it appears that Supervisor Alioto
may use campaign funds to pay for the travel expenses of Francisco
Hsieh and Mitch Fine to China.
9
Section 89513 (b), which deals with expenditures for
professional services, also applies to Mitch Fine.
The section
provides that “[c)ainpaign funds shall not be used to pay for or
reimburse the cost of professional services unless the services
are directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.”
Based on the information you provided in your letter of
November 12,
1993, the committee may pay for the legal services of
Mr. Fine.

Alioto,

and by other

Original 3.
Can Angela Alioto’s officeholder account be used
to pay for any portion of the expenses of this trip for
(a) Angela
Alioto,
(b)
members of her household who live with her,
(C) the
head of the San Francisco Zoo,
(d)
members of the press,
(e)
members of the business community,
(f)

Francisco Zoological Society,
a nonprofit organization organized
to help support the Zoo,
(g)
Angela Alioto’s press secretary
(campaign staff),
and

Revised 3.
May Angela Alioto use campaign funds in her
officeholder account to pay for any portion of the expenses of
this trip for
(a)
Angela Alioto,
(b) her mother
(who lives with
her),
(c)
Mr.
Hal Markowitz
(Director of Research at the San
Francisco Zoo),
(d)
Marc Provissiero (her campaign staff press
secretary),
(e)
Mr. Francisco Hsieh (her liason to the Asian
community),
and
(f)
Mr. Mitch Fine (her legal and political
advisor)?
given during the week of July 12,
1993.
We discussed the various
issues on four different occasions in an attempt to clarify any
misunderstandings,
namely,
on August 3,
1993,
and again on
September 13,
17,
and 21 or 22,
1993.
Those conversations
resulted in additional information being submitted to this office
by facsimile on September 14 and 17 and again on November 12,
1993,
by a separate request for written advice,
our file number
A-93—441, consolidated in our reply herein.


Our File Nos.
A—93—309/A—93441

CONCLUSION: 1.
Supervisor Alioto may accept the Chengdu Province
Government’s offer to pay for her travel,
including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence during her trip
to China.
The payment of these travel expenses on her behalf are
not subject to the statutory $1,000 gift limit of the Act.
Moreover, Supervisor Alioto may accept the Chengdu Province
Government’s offer to pay for the travel expenses of four persons
designated by her to accompany her on the trip to China.
However,
these payments will be subject to the $1,000 gift limit.
3
2.
Regardless of whether a gift is subject to the $1,000
limit or exempt from the limit, absent an express exception to the
definition of “gift” in Section 82028, the receipt of free
transportation,
lodging,
and reasonable subsistence would be
disciosable on Supervisor Alioto’s anriual.Statement of Economic
Interests.
The donor is the Chengdu Province Government.
Likewise,
any of the four persons who are designated by
Supervisor Alioto to receive free transportation,
lodging,
and
reasonable subsistence within China would disclose these gifts on
their annual Statement of Economic Interests, if they are public
officials under the Act.
The donor is Angela Alioto.
3.
Supervisor Alioto may use campaign funds to pay for her
own transportation expenses to China as well as for the
transportation expenses to China of her mother,
Marc Provissiero,
Hal Markowitz, Francisco Hsieh and Mitch Fine.
However,
she may
not use campaign funds to pay for all of their expenses, but only
for those expenses that are directly related to a political,
legislative,
or governmental purpose.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission December 23, 1993 Peter Bagatelos BAGATELOS & FADEM 601 California Street Suite 1801 San Francisco, CA 94108 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File Nos. A—93—309/A—93—441 Dear Mr. Bagatelos: You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided to you pursuant to the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) . The advice was requested on behalf of San Francisco Supervisor Angela Alioto concerning her proposed trip to China. This letter confirms the oral advice I gave Marc Provissiero and summarizes the three telephone conversations we had subsequent to the receipt of your initial letter of August 10, 1993.2 In 1 Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 As I advised you in September (see below), your letter of August 10, 1993, requesting confirmation of the oral advice I had provided to Marc Provissiero during the week of July 12, 1993, is not entirely accurate. Mr. Provissiero asked us four questions, namely, (1) May Supervisor Alioto accept payment of her travel, including actual domestic transportation and related lodging and subsistence within China; (2) Is there a difference between Supervisor Alioto and the persons she designates to accompany her; (3) May her staff or campaign workers who will be accompanying her on the trip receive gifts; and (4) May her Friends Account or campaign pay for the travel and lodging expenses for her staff and campaign workers who will be accompanying her on the trip. Briefly, I answered (1) Yes, 18950.2(c); (2) Yes, Supervisor Alioto is the source of the gifts for persons accompanying her (Regulation 18945) whereas foreign country is the source of the gifts to her; (3) Yes, Regulation 18950.2(b); (4) It depends upon specific person, Section 89513(a) or Section 89513(f). In your initial letter of August 10, 1993, you rephrased the initial inquiry, consolidated the four questions into three questions, and thus did not accurately summarize the oral advice 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File Nos. A—93—309/A—93--441 Page 2 addition, this letter shall serve as a reply to your most recent written request for advice, our file number A-93-441. This letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Ocrlesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) While you did provide this office with supplemental information and argument as to why Supervisor Alioto should be permitted to use her campaign funds to pay the travel expenses of Mr. Mitch Fine so that he could accompany her on the trip to China, at no time did you provide us with an itinerary or a proposed schedule of activities for Supervisor Alioto or any of her entourage. Therefore, our reply to your questions concerning
